label: delphi-r2-p3
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would hold that the councilmembers' Signal messages concerning the franchise were public records under the CPRA, and that the city's "no responsive records" response, made without any inquiry to the members about personal-account communications, violated the Act."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 76,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "A court would very likely find the Signal messages qualified as \"public records\" under City of San Jose (squarely on-point precedent on personal-device communications about public business), and would somewhat less certainly but still probably find that the clerk's \"no responsive records\" response violated the Act, because the search never reached the councilmembers' personal accounts and because roughly 40 known-to-exist screenshots were never located or produced at all.",
  "rationales": [
    "City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 held that writings concerning the conduct of public business remain public records regardless of storage on personal devices/accounts, and franchise-term negotiations, diversion targets, and RFP timing plainly concern the conduct of public business, making the substantive 'public record' determination close to settled on these facts.",
    "The clerk's search was confined to city systems and never reached out to the three councilmembers about personal-account communications, which is hard to reconcile with San Jose's remand direction and with the post-San Jose statutory codification (AB 700, now generally cited as Gov. Code section 7922.545 - verify exact number) requiring agencies to route requests reasonably likely to implicate personal accounts to the individual official for a diligent search.",
    "The undisputed existence of ~40 surviving screenshots in a councilmember's possession that were neither searched for nor disclosed is itself strong evidence that 'no responsive records' was factually inaccurate, independent of the harder legal question about the already-deleted messages.",
    "CPRA declaratory and injunctive relief (Gov. Code section 7923.100, formerly section 6259) does not require that responsive records presently exist, so a court can declare the deleted messages were public records and that the response process violated the Act even though most content is now unrecoverable, which supports finding a violation despite the auto-deletion.",
    "Countervailing uncertainty: the city could argue the clerk had no actual or constructive knowledge of the Signal group's existence at the time of the CPRA response, and no controlling appellate decision has yet defined precisely how much affirmative inquiry into elected officials' ephemeral personal messaging apps 'reasonable search' requires, giving a trial court room to find the search adequate under the circumstances then known."
  ],
  "authorities": [
    "City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608",
    "Gov. Code section 7922.500 (formerly section 6253) - duty to make public records available upon request",
    "Gov. Code section 7922.545 (added by AB 700, Stats. 2021, ch. 132) - obligations regarding search of personal files/accounts for responsive records (verify exact section number and text)",
    "Gov. Code section 7923.100 (formerly section 6259) - court authority to order disclosure and grant declaratory/injunctive relief in CPRA actions",
    "Gov. Code section 34090 - authorization and conditions for destruction of city records (relevant primarily to the retention-duty sub-question, not the core public-records characterization)"
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether a court will treat the duty to inquire into councilmembers' personal accounts as automatically triggered by a broadly worded CPRA request (making the clerk's failure to ask a per se violation), or will instead excuse the clerk's search as reasonable absent proof the clerk had actual or constructive notice of the Signal group at the time of the response - this precise 'trigger' question has not been definitively resolved by binding published California appellate authority since San Jose."
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 78.0, mean 76.9, std dev 3.2, range 70.0–80.0, middle 50% of panel between 76.0 and 80.0.

## 2. Rationale Themes

**Theme A — Content-controls-not-location (San Jose prong one).** *7 of 7 panelists.* Every panelist grounded the "public record" determination in *City of San Jose*'s content-based test, and every panelist characterized the subject matter (franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will accept," RFP timing) as core conduct of the public's business.

**Theme B — Duty to inquire of individual custodians / reasonable-search standard.** *7 of 7 panelists.* All invoked some version of a reasonably-calculated or reasonable-diligence search standard and noted the clerk's failure to ask any of the three councilmembers about personal-account communications. Panelists differed on the doctrinal source (San Jose remand instructions, § 6253.1/§ 7922.600 duty-to-assist, AB 700, case law on search adequacy) — see Theme E and Disagreement point 2.

**Theme C — "Any platform or account" request language as notice.** *5 of 7 panelists* (probabilities 70, 76, 78, 78, 80). These panelists argued the request's express wording put the clerk on notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient, with some describing the resulting search as "facially inadequate" or a candidate for a per-se violation.

**Theme D — The ~40 surviving screenshots.** *7 of 7 panelists, but used in two distinct ways:*
- *6 panelists* (76 through 80) treated the screenshots as making "no responsive records" factually inaccurate at the moment it was given — an independent basis for a violation regardless of the deleted messages.
- *1 panelist* (70) used the screenshots differently: as evidentiary proof of content and participation that allows a court to reach a definitive holding on the public-record question, rather than as proof of response falsity.

**Theme E — AB 700 statutory codification of personal-account search duties.** *2 of 7 panelists* (76, 78) cited AB 700 as codifying an affirmative duty to route requests to individual officials — though with different section numbers and chapter citations (see Disagreement point 5).

**Theme F — Conjunctive fragility of the proposition.** *5 of 7 panelists* (70, 76, 78, 78, 80) expressly noted that the proposition requires both prongs to hold, making it more fragile than either prong alone; the second (search-adequacy) prong was uniformly identified as the weaker link among these panelists.

**Theme G — Clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge as a countervailing defense.** *3 of 7 panelists* (76, 76, 78) — a mid-range cluster — flagged that a court could excuse the initial search as reasonable if the clerk had no actual or constructive notice of the Signal group at the time of response.

**Theme H — Majority-bloc participation strengthens the public-business nexus.** *3 of 7 panelists* (78, 80, 80) — skewing toward higher-probability panelists — emphasized that three of five councilmembers (a voting majority) participating in substantive deliberation reinforces both the content nexus and the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element.

**Theme I — Deleted-messages scope problem.** *2 of 7 panelists* (both at 80 — the highest-probability panelists) raised the possibility that a court might limit its "public records" holding to the surviving screenshots and treat the bulk of auto-deleted messages as a retention/spoliation issue rather than a CPRA determination.

**Theme J — Availability of declaratory relief despite destruction.** *1 panelist* (76) noted that CPRA declaratory/injunctive relief does not require that responsive records presently exist.

**Theme K — Possible exemptions even if records qualify.** *1 panelist* (76) noted content could be found preliminary/deliberative and exempt from disclosure despite public-record status.

**Theme L — Third-party/consultant-hosted platform distinction.** *1 panelist* (70 — the lowest-probability panelist) flagged that San Jose involved the official's own texts/emails, whereas this involves a private bidder's consultant-run disappearing-message platform, with no published California decision on point.

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **What triggers the duty to inquire of individual officials.** Some panelists treat the request's "any platform or account" language as itself sufficient to trigger the duty (making the city-systems-only search per se or facially inadequate); others posit that absent actual or constructive knowledge of the Signal group, the search could be found reasonable "at the time." Several panelists hold both positions in tension within their own answers, but as between panelists the trigger assumption is incompatible: request-language-alone versus knowledge-required.

2. **The state of post-San Jose search-methodology doctrine.** The 70 panelist cites the 2018 Court of Appeal remand decision (*City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013) as published authority contemplating that agencies must ask custodians to search personal accounts. Other panelists (78, 78, 80) describe search methodology as expressly left unresolved by San Jose, with no binding appellate authority fixing the duty's contours. These are incompatible readings of the same doctrinal landscape.

3. **Scope of any "public records" holding as to the deleted messages.** The two 80 panelists assume a court might confine its holding to the ~40 surviving screenshots and recharacterize the rest as a retention/spoliation matter; other panelists' analyses assume the full body of Signal messages is subject to the public-record determination.

4. **Whether the Signal platform's characteristics matter.** The 70 panelist treats the consultant-initiated, third-party-hosted, ephemeral nature of the channel as a genuine open question under the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element; other panelists (78, 80) describe these facts as a *cleaner* fit for San Jose than San Jose's own facts, treating the platform's characteristics as immaterial.

5. **What Gov. Code § 7922.545 is.** One panelist (76) cites § 7922.545 as the AB 700 personal-account search provision (Stats. 2021, ch. 132); another panelist (76) cites § 7922.545 as the recodified preliminary-drafts exemption (former § 6254(a)); a third panelist (78) cites the AB 700 provision as § 6252.5 (Stats. 2021, ch. 456). These citations are mutually inconsistent as to section number and chapter, and multiple panelists flagged their own citations for verification.

6. **Per se violation versus fact-bound adequacy question.** Panelists split on whether the "no responsive records" response would be treated as a clean statutory violation or as a fact-intensive search-adequacy question on which the city could partially prevail through a good-faith showing.

## 4. Authorities Cited

**Cited by two or more panelists:**
- *City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 — **7 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 34090 (local agency records retention/destruction) — **7 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6252(e) / § 7920.530 (definition of "public records") — **4 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6253.1 / § 7922.600 (duty to assist requester) — **3 panelists**
- *ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court* (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 (search adequacy) — **3 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6253 / § 7922.500 / § 7922.530 (duty to make records available) — **3 panelists**
- AB 700 personal-account search provisions (cited variously as § 7922.545 and § 6252.5) — **2 panelists** (with inconsistent section/chapter citations; see Disagreement point 5)
- CPRA judicial-enforcement provisions (§ 7923.100, cited variously as former § 6259 and former § 6258) — **2 panelists**

**Cited by one panelist each:**
- *City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 (Court of Appeal remand decision)
- *Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385
- *Anderson-Barker v. Superior Court* (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 528 (flagged by the citing panelist as an analog to verify)
- Gov. Code § 7920.545 / former § 6252(g) (definition of "writing")
- Gov. Code § 7922.545 / former § 6254(a) (cited as preliminary drafts/notes exemption)
- Gov. Code § 7923.115 / former § 6259 (fee-shifting)
- Gov. Code § 7920.000 et seq. (general CPRA recodification citation)

Several panelists flagged their own recodified section numbers for verification; those flags are preserved above without correction.

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":78,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"A court would very likely hold the Signal messages were \"public records\" under City of San Jose's content-controls-not-location test (franchise terms, diversion targets, RFP timing, and \"what council will accept\" are core public business, and a voting-majority bloc of three councilmembers substantively participated), and would more likely than not also hold that the clerk's \"no responsive records\" response violated the Act, because the request's explicit \"any platform or account\" language put the city on notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient, no inquiry was ever routed to the individual councilmembers, and roughly 40 surviving screenshots demonstrably responsive to the request were never located or produced. I do not think a court confining detailed remedial relief to the 40 surviving screenshots would necessarily decline to characterize the broader Signal exchange as having been public records while it existed, so the 'scope-narrowing' concern raised by some analyses goes more to remedy than to the characterization question the proposition actually poses.","Countervailing uncertainty that keeps me from going higher: there is no binding published appellate decision squarely holding that a consultant-initiated, third-party-hosted, auto-deleting group chat triggers the same search obligations as San Jose's personal texts/emails, and a trial court could plausibly credit a good-faith, reasonable-search defense for the pre-litigation response given the clerk's lack of actual knowledge of the group's existence, particularly since the exact statutory trigger for the AB 700 personal-account-inquiry duty (request-scope alone vs. some threshold of agency awareness) remains doctrinally unsettled."],"authorities":["City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608","Gov. Code section 7922.500 / former section 6253 - duty to make public records available upon request","Gov. Code section 7922.600 / former section 6253.1 - duty to assist requester in identifying records and clarifying requests","AB 700 personal-account search provisions, Stats. 2021 (exact recodified section number disputed among analyses -- cited variously as section 7922.545 and section 6252.5; verify correct citation and text)","ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 - reasonable search / search-adequacy standard","Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385 - agency must conduct search reasonably calculated to locate responsive records","Gov. Code section 7923.100 / former section 6259 - court authority to order disclosure and grant declaratory/injunctive relief in CPRA enforcement actions","Gov. Code section 34090 - authorization and conditions for destruction of local agency records (relevant to retention sub-question, not core public-record characterization)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether the duty to inquire of individual councilmembers about personal-account communications is triggered automatically by a broadly worded CPRA request (making the city-systems-only search a clean violation), or instead requires some showing of the clerk's actual or constructive knowledge of the specific Signal group before the search can be deemed unreasonable -- this precise trigger question lacks controlling published California appellate authority post-San Jose, and the panel itself could not agree on the correct statutory citation or content of the AB 700 provision that might resolve it.","changed":true,"response_to_panel":"Two panel considerations genuinely moved me, modestly, from 76 to 78. First, Theme C (the request's explicit \"any platform or account\" language as notice) sharpened my own reasoning: on reflection I think this language is best read as triggering the AB 700-style duty to route the request to individual officials regardless of the clerk's independent knowledge of the Signal group, which pushes the search-adequacy prong somewhat higher than I credited in round 1, where I treated the knowledge question as more central. Second, Theme H (majority-bloc participation) reinforced my confidence on the 'public record' prong, since three of five members deliberating together is a materially stronger public-business nexus than San Jose's own facts. I did not adopt Theme I's scope-narrowing concern (confining any holding to the 40 screenshots) as a reason to lower my number, because I read the proposition as posing a characterization question the general exchange, not a remedy question, and I think a court would resolve the characterization question for the class of communications much as San Jose did. I also declined to adopt the 2018 Court of Appeal citation offered by one panelist (24 Cal.App.5th 1013) without being able to verify it, and flagged the AB 700 section-number dispute (Theme/Disagreement 5) as unresolved rather than picking a side. I stayed within the panel's tight range rather than anchoring to the median mechanically -- 78 reflects my own reconstructed prong-by-prong estimate (~90% public-record prong x ~83-85% search-violation prong, adjusted upward for correlation between the two findings) rather than deference to the group's central tendency."}

data.position_summary: A court would very likely hold the Signal messages were "public records" under City of San Jose's content-controls-not-location test (franchise terms, diversion targets, RFP timing, and "what council will accept" are core public business, and a voting-majority bloc of three councilmembers substantively participated), and would more likely than not also hold that the clerk's "no responsive records" response violated the Act, because the request's explicit "any platform or account" language put the city on notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient, no inquiry was ever routed to the individual councilmembers, and roughly 40 surviving screenshots demonstrably responsive to the request were never located or produced. The weaker link remains the search-adequacy prong, given the absence of binding appellate authority precisely fixing what triggers the duty to inquire of individual officials about personal-account communications.
rationales: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 makes the 'public record' characterization close to settled on these facts; if anything this scenario is a cleaner fit than San Jose's own facts because it involves a sustained, multi-member deliberative exchange among a voting-majority bloc (3 of 5) about a specific $180M procurement, which strengthens both the 'concerning the conduct of public business' and 'prepared, used, or retained by a public official' elements relative to San Jose's individual-employee-text facts.

The request's express 'on any platform or account' language is a strong, independent basis for finding the city-systems-only search inadequate: it forecloses any argument that the city lacked notice personal accounts were in scope, which is precisely the gap AB 700 (Stats. 2021, ch. 132 or 300 - verify) was designed to close by requiring agencies to forward such requests to individual officials/employees for a diligent personal-account search; on this reading the duty to inquire is triggered by request scope, not by the clerk's actual/constructive knowledge of the specific Signal group.

The undisputed existence of ~40 surviving screenshots in a councilmember's possession, never searched for or disclosed, independently establishes that 'no responsive records' was factually false at the moment it was given -- a violation that does not depend on resolving the harder legal question of whether the already-deleted messages retained public-record status, and that a court could find even if it treated the deleted-message body more cautiously.

The proposition's phrasing ('were public records... concerning the franchise') most naturally reads as a legal characterization of the communications as a class, similar to how San Jose itself announced a governing principle rather than adjudicating each individual text; I do not think a court confining detailed remedial relief to the 40 surviving screenshots would necessarily decline to characterize the broader Signal exchange as having been public records while it existed, so the 'scope-narrowing' concern raised by some analyses goes more to remedy than to the characterization question the proposition actually poses.

Countervailing uncertainty that keeps me from going higher: there is no binding published appellate decision squarely holding that a consultant-initiated, third-party-hosted, auto-deleting group chat triggers the same search obligations as San Jose's personal texts/emails, and a trial court could plausibly credit a good-faith, reasonable-search defense for the pre-litigation response given the clerk's lack of actual knowledge of the group's existence, particularly since the exact statutory trigger for the AB 700 personal-account-inquiry duty (request-scope alone vs. some threshold of agency awareness) remains doctrinally unsettled.
authorities: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608
Gov. Code section 7922.500 / former section 6253 - duty to make public records available upon request
Gov. Code section 7922.600 / former section 6253.1 - duty to assist requester in identifying records and clarifying requests

AB 700 personal-account search provisions, Stats. 2021 (exact recodified section number disputed among analyses -- cited variously as section 7922.545 and section 6252.5; verify correct citation and text)
ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 - reasonable search / search-adequacy standard
Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385 - agency must conduct search reasonably calculated to locate responsive records
Gov. Code section 7923.100 / former section 6259 - court authority to order disclosure and grant declaratory/injunctive relief in CPRA enforcement actions
Gov. Code section 34090 - authorization and conditions for destruction of local agency records (relevant to retention sub-question, not core public-record characterization)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the duty to inquire of individual councilmembers about personal-account communications is triggered automatically by a broadly worded CPRA request (making the city-systems-only search a clean violation), or instead requires some showing of the clerk's actual or constructive knowledge of the specific Signal group before the search can be deemed unreasonable -- this precise trigger question lacks controlling published California appellate authority post-San Jose, and the panel itself could not agree on the correct statutory citation or content of the AB 700 provision that might resolve it.
data.response_to_panel: Two panel considerations genuinely moved me, modestly, from 76 to 78. First, Theme C (the request's explicit "any platform or account" language as notice) sharpened my own reasoning: on reflection I think this language is best read as triggering the AB 700-style duty to route the request to individual officials regardless of the clerk's independent knowledge of the Signal group, which pushes the search-adequacy prong somewhat higher than I credited in round 1, where I treated the knowledge question as more central. Second, Theme H (majority-bloc participation) reinforced my confidence on the 'public record' prong, since three of five members deliberating together is a materially stronger public-business nexus than San Jose's own facts. I did not adopt Theme I's scope-narrowing concern (confining any holding to the 40 screenshots) as a reason to lower my number, because I read the proposition as posing a characterization question the general exchange, not a remedy question, and I think a court would resolve the characterization question for the class of communications much as San Jose did. I also declined to adopt the 2018 Court of Appeal citation offered by one panelist (24 Cal.App.5th 1013) without being able to verify it, and flagged the AB 700 section-number dispute (Theme/Disagreement 5) as unresolved rather than picking a side. I stayed within the panel's tight range rather than anchoring to the median mechanically -- 78 reflects my own reconstructed prong-by-prong estimate (~90% public-record prong x ~83-85% search-violation prong, adjusted upward for correlation between the two findings) rather than deference to the group's central tendency.
